html:
[201]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 唐參軍 - Title referring to a low-ranking official in the Tang dynasty.
  • 立性修整，簡於接對 - Describes Tang Sanjun's character and social behavior: upright in character and well-disciplined, frugal or selective in social interactions.
  • 投刺 - Formal way of presenting a visiting card or letter of introduction.
  • 點心飯 - Ambiguous term that could refer to a light meal or snack, but in this context seems to be a euphemism for a small favor or request.
  • 門者不報 - Indicates that the gatekeeper did not report the visitors, a common practice in formal settings.
  • 引出外廳 - Describes the action of leading the visitors out to the outer hall, a common practice to handle visitors who were not properly announced.
  • 私誡奴 - Describes a private instruction given to the servant, which must be understood in the context of the household's internal communication.
  • 寘劍盤中 - Symbolic act of preparing for violence, which must be read in the context of the impending attack.
  • 中之 - Indicates that the attack was successful, which must be read in the context of the story's progression.
  • 猶躍入庭前池中 - Describes the aftermath of the attack, which must be read in the context of the story's resolution.
  • 彼我雖是狐 - Introduces the supernatural nature of the characters, which must be read in the context of the story's fantastical elements.
  • 千年之狐 - Describes a long-lived and powerful supernatural being, which must be read in the context of the story's supernatural elements.
  • 姓趙姓張 - Describes the lineage of the fox, which must be read in the context of the story's supernatural elements.
  • 五百年狐 - Describes another type of supernatural being, which must be read in the context of the story's supernatural elements.
  • 姓白姓康 - Describes the lineage of the second fox, which must be read in the context of the story's supernatural elements.
  • 奈何無道 - Moral rebuke, which must be read in the context of the story's moral and ethical dimensions.
  • 殺我康三必當修報於汝 - Threat of vengeance, which must be read in the context of the story's conflict and resolution.
  • 終不令康氏子徒死也 - Promise of justice, which must be read in the context of the story's resolution.
  • 深謝之 - Indicates gratitude, which must be read in the context of the story's resolution.
  • 召康三 - Describes the action of summoning the injured fox, which must be read in the context of the story's resolution.
  • 桃湯沃灑 - Traditional method to ward off evil spirits, which must be read in the context of the story's resolution.
  • 懸符禁 - Another method to protect against supernatural threats, which must be read in the context of the story's resolution.
  • 施行有驗 - Indicates that the protective measures worked, which must be read in the context of the story's resolution.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 唐參軍 - Refers to a low-ranking official in the Tang dynasty, indicating the setting of the story in the Tang period.
  • 狐 - In Chinese folklore, represents cunning and supernatural beings. The story involves two foxes, one of whom is a thousand-year-old and the other a five-hundred-year-old.
  • 桃湯 - Traditional Chinese remedy believed to ward off evil spirits and protect against supernatural threats.
  • 符禁 - Traditional Chinese magical symbols and spells used for protection and exorcism.

Proper Nouns

  • 唐參軍 - Title referring to a low-ranking official in the Tang dynasty.
  • 趙門福 - Name of one of the visitors, who is a supernatural being.
  • 康三 - Name of the second visitor, who is also a supernatural being.
  • 洛陽 - City in the Tang dynasty, the setting of the story.
  • 思恭里 - Specific area within Luoyang, the location of Tang Sanjun's residence.
  • 桃湯 - Traditional remedy for warding off evil spirits.
  • 符禁 - Traditional magical symbols and spells used for protection and exorcism.

[202]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 久之 - Indicates a passage of time, often translated as 'after some time' or 'after a long while.'
  • 暇日 - Means 'leisure time' or 'free day,' referring to a time when one is not occupied with work.
  • 忽見 - Means 'suddenly saw,' indicating an unexpected encounter.
  • 聊复采食 - Means 'just came to pick and eat,' suggesting a casual or playful action.
  • 君亦食之否 - A question meaning 'will you eat it too?' where '君' is a respectful term for 'you.'
  • 频掷数四 - Means 'threw several times,' indicating repeated actions.
  • 愈恐 - Means 'became even more afraid,' showing increasing fear or concern.
  • 广召僧 - Means 'widely summoned monks,' indicating a large-scale call for help.
  • 结坛持咒 - Means 'established an altar and chanted spells,' referring to a ritualistic act.
  • 逾日不至 - Means 'did not come for several days,' indicating a prolonged absence.
  • 持诵甚切 - Means 'chanted with great earnestness,' emphasizing the intensity of the monks' devotion.
  • 冀其有效 - Means 'hoped it would be effective,' showing the expectation of results.
  • 以为己功 - Means 'to claim it as their own achievement,' indicating a desire for personal recognition.
  • 晚霁之后 - Means 'after the evening cleared up,' referring to a time after rain had stopped.
  • 五色云自西来 - Means 'a multicolored cloud came from the west,' describing a colorful cloud.
  • 迳至唐氏堂前 - Means 'directly arrived at the front of the Tang family's hall,' indicating a direct path.
  • 容色端严 - Means 'with a dignified appearance,' describing the Buddha's demeanor.
  • 稽首 - A formal bow, often translated as 'prostrated themselves.'
  • 虔礼甚至 - Means 'worshipped with great reverence,' indicating deep respect.
  • 喜见真佛 - Means 'gladly saw the true Buddha,' showing joy and reverence.
  • 拜请降止 - Means 'bowed and invited to descend,' indicating a request for the Buddha to come down.
  • 坐其坛上 - Means 'sat on the altar,' indicating the Buddha's presence.
  • 奉事甚勤 - Means 'served with great diligence,' showing the family's dedication.
  • 汝是修道，请通达 - Means 'you are practicing the Way, please understand,' addressing the monk.
  • 亦何须久蔬食 - Means 'why must you eat vegetables for a long time,' questioning the necessity of a vegetarian diet.
  • 而为法能食肉乎 - Means 'can you eat meat for the sake of the Dharma?' questioning the monk's dietary practices.
  • 但问心能坚持否 - Means 'but ask if your heart can remain steadfast,' emphasizing the importance of mental discipline.
  • 肉虽食之，可复无累 - Means 'even if you eat meat, it will not cause harm,' indicating that eating meat does not necessarily lead to negative consequences.
  • 乃令唐氏市肉 - Means 'then ordered the Tang family to buy meat,' indicating a command.
  • 佛自设食 - Means 'the Buddha set the food,' indicating the Buddha's involvement in the meal.
  • 次以授僧及家人 - Means 'then gave it to the monk and the family,' indicating the distribution of food.
  • 悉食 - Means 'all ate,' indicating that everyone partook of the meal.
  • 忽见坛上是赵门福 - Means 'suddenly saw Zhao Menfu on the altar,' indicating an unexpected revelation.
  • 举家叹恨 - Means 'the whole family sighed with regret,' showing disappointment and anger.
  • 无劳厌我 - Means 'do not bother to dislike me,' indicating a plea not to be disliked.
  • 我不来矣 - Means 'I will not come again,' indicating a final departure.
  • 自尔不至也 - Means 'from then on, he did not come again,' indicating a permanent absence.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 唐氏夫妻 - Refers to the Tang couple, a common family name in Chinese literature.
  • 赵门福 - Refers to Zhao Menfu, a character in the story.
  • 五色云 - Refers to a multicolored cloud, often associated with divine or supernatural phenomena.
  • 佛 - Refers to the Buddha, a central figure in Buddhism.
  • 结坛持咒 - Refers to a ritualistic act involving the establishment of an altar and chanting spells, common in Buddhist practices.
  • 真佛 - Refers to the true Buddha, emphasizing the authenticity of the divine presence.
  • 修道 - Refers to the practice of the Way, often associated with Buddhist or Daoist practices.
  • 蔬食 - Refers to a vegetarian diet, common in Buddhist monastic practices.
  • 肉 - Refers to meat, which is generally avoided in Buddhist monastic practices.
  • 市肉 - Refers to buying meat, indicating a departure from traditional Buddhist dietary practices.
  • 设食 - Refers to setting the food, indicating the preparation of a meal.
  • 举家 - Refers to the whole family, emphasizing the collective reaction.
  • 叹恨 - Refers to sighing with regret, showing a mix of disappointment and anger.
  • 无劳厌我 - Refers to a plea not to be disliked, indicating a desire to maintain a positive relationship.
  • 自尔不至也 - Refers to a permanent absence, indicating that Zhao Menfu will not return.

Proper Nouns

  • 唐氏 - The Tang family.
  • 赵门福 - Zhao Menfu.
  • 佛 - The Buddha.
  • 僧 - Monks.
  • 唐氏堂前 - The front of the Tang family's hall.
  • 五色云 - A multicolored cloud.
  • 楹前 - In front of the pillars.
  • 野狐 - A wild fox, often used as a metaphor in Chinese literature.
  • 真佛 - The true Buddha.
  • 修道 - The practice of the Way.
  • 蔬食 - Vegetarian diet.
  • 肉 - Meat.
  • 市肉 - Buying meat.
  • 设食 - Setting the food.
  • 举家 - The whole family.
  • 叹恨 - Sighing with regret.
  • 无劳厌我 - A plea not to be disliked.
  • 自尔不至也 - A permanent absence, indicating that Zhao Menfu will not return.

[203]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 嚴谏 - Personal name, should be kept consistent in translation.
  • 従叔 - Refers to a maternal uncle or cousin on the father's side, depending on context.
  • 吊之 - To mourn or pay respects to the deceased.
  • 去服 - To remove mourning clothes, indicating the end of the mourning period.
  • 有如平生 - As if alive, describing the spirit or ghost acting as it did when alive.
  • 料理 - To handle or deal with something, often in a practical or administrative sense.
  • 逆怒 - To be angry in return or to show anger in response.
  • 约束子弟 - To restrain or discipline children or young people.
  • 勿更令少府侄来 - Do not again make the nephew of the high-ranking official come.
  • 无益人家事 - No benefit to family affairs.
  • 只解相疑耳 - Only understand to doubt each other.
  • 五郎公事似忙 - The official business of the high-ranking official seems busy.
  • 不宜数来也 - Should not come frequently.
  • 苍鹰、双鹘、皂雕、猎犬 - Types of birds and dogs used for hunting.
  • 手力 - Laborers or workers.
  • 器械 - Weapons or tools.
  • 围绕其宅数重 - To surround his house several layers or rounds.
  • 赤肉野狐 - Red meat wild fox.
  • 仰行屋上 - To walk upside down on the roof.
  • 射击不能中 - Cannot hit when shooting.
  • 跃出 - To leap out.
  • 怪绝 - Extremely strange or bizarre.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 唐 - Refers to the Tang Dynasty, a period of significant cultural and literary development in Chinese history.
  • 洛阳尉 - A local government official in Luoyang, the capital of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 野狐 - In Chinese folklore, wild foxes are often associated with supernatural beings or spirits.
  • 少府 - A high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty, often responsible for various administrative tasks.
  • 苍鹰、双鹘、皂雕、猎犬 - Specific types of birds and dogs used for hunting, which were common in the Tang Dynasty.

Proper Nouns

  • 严谏 - A personal name, should be kept consistent.
  • 従叔 - A family relationship, should be translated as 'maternal uncle' or 'cousin on the father's side.'
  • 洛阳 - The capital of the Tang Dynasty, should be translated as 'Luoyang.'
  • 少府 - A title for a high-ranking official, should be translated as 'high-ranking official.'
  • 苍鹰、双鹘、皂雕、猎犬 - Specific types of birds and dogs, should be translated as 'gray eagle, double falcon, black eagle, and hunting dog.'

[204]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 隱德 - Hidden virtue or moral excellence that is not widely recognized.
  • 不慧 - Means 'not intelligent' or 'lacking wisdom,' used here to contrast with the protagonist's hidden virtue.
  • 財帛當以道 - Means 'wealth and property should be acquired through the right way,' emphasizing ethical conduct in wealth acquisition.
  • 可取之 - Means 'can be taken,' indicating the possibility of obtaining the treasure.
  • 有定分 - Means 'there is a fixed destiny or fate,' suggesting that some things are beyond human control.
  • 妙法 - Refers to a profound or mysterious method or principle.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 唐潤州參軍 - Refers to a military officer in the Tang dynasty, specifically from Runzhou (modern-day Zhenjiang, Jiangsu).
  • 昆明池 - A large artificial lake in the Han dynasty capital, Chang'an, often associated with legends of treasure.
  • 開封縣 - Refers to Kaifeng County, a place name.
  • 狐媚 - Refers to fox spirits or fox demons, often associated with supernatural phenomena in Chinese folklore.
  • 術士 - Refers to practitioners of magic or medicine, often with supernatural abilities.
  • 不能療 - Means 'could not cure,' indicating the failure of previous attempts to treat the condition.

Proper Nouns

  • 唐潤州參軍 - Refers to a military officer in the Tang dynasty, specifically from Runzhou (modern-day Zhenjiang, Jiangsu).
  • 昆明池 - Refers to a large artificial lake in the Han dynasty capital, Chang'an.
  • 開封縣 - Refers to Kaifeng County.
  • 狐媚 - Refers to fox spirits or fox demons.
  • 術士 - Refers to practitioners of magic or medicine.

[205]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 有道士者善见鬼 - Literally means 'there is a Taoist who is good at seeing ghosts.' Introduces a Taoist with supernatural abilities.
  • 若得此人，太夫人疾苦必愈 - Conditional statement expressing the hope that the Taoist can cure the magistrate's mother.
  • 遣候之 - Means 'sent someone to wait for him,' indicating the magistrate sent someone to track down the Taoist.
  • 宜自谒见 - Suggests that the Taoist should be approached directly.
  • 具申礼请 - Indicates a formal and respectful invitation.
  • 笑曰 - Conveys the Taoist's demeanor with a smile.
  • 屈身于人，亦可悯矣 - Expresses the Taoist's sympathy for the magistrate's situation.
  • 幸与君遇，其疾必愈 - Indicates the Taoist's willingness to help.
  • 自县桥至宅，可少止人，令百姓见之 - Suggests that the Taoist should be visible to the public.
  • 我当至彼为发遣 - Indicates that the Taoist will perform a ritual to send away the ghost.
  • 且宜还家洒扫，焚香相待 - Indicates preparation for the Taoist's arrival.
  • 皆如其言 - Indicates that everything happened as the Taoist predicted.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 道士 - Refers to a Taoist, a practitioner of Taoism, often associated with supernatural abilities and rituals.
  • 太夫人 - Refers to the magistrate's mother, a term of respect for an elderly woman.
  • 县桥 - Refers to a bridge in the county, a common landmark in Chinese towns.
  • 鬼 - Refers to a ghost or spirit, a common theme in Chinese folklore and religion.
  • 发遣 - Refers to a ritual to send away a ghost, a common practice in Taoist and folk rituals.

Proper Nouns

  • 道士 - The term for a Taoist, a practitioner of Taoism.
  • 令 - The term for the magistrate, the local administrative official.
  • 太夫人 - The term for the magistrate's mother, a term of respect.
  • 县桥 - The term for a bridge in the county, a common landmark.
  • 鬼 - The term for a ghost or spirit, a common theme in Chinese folklore.
  • 发遣 - The term for a ritual to send away a ghost, a common practice in Taoist and folk rituals.

[207]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 代州民 - Refers to the people of Dai Prefecture, a historical region in northern China.
  • 乘云而至 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to arrive riding on clouds,' often used to describe the appearance of deities or supernatural beings.
  • 汝家甚善 - A polite expression meaning 'your home is very good,' used here to indicate approval or favor.
  • 不令有言 - Means 'do not allow anyone to speak,' indicating a desire for secrecy or confidentiality.
  • 四方信心 - Refers to people from all directions who have faith or belief, suggesting widespread influence or popularity.
  • 私通有娠 - Literally means 'secretly having intercourse and becoming pregnant,' used here to describe a forbidden relationship.
  • 倾财求道士 - Means 'spending all one's wealth to seek a Taoist priest,' indicating desperation or urgency.
  • 窃视菩萨 - Means 'to secretly look at the Bodhisattva,' suggesting a hidden or covert action.
  • 老狐 - Literally means 'old fox,' often used in Chinese folklore to describe cunning or deceitful beings.
  • 不欲见男子 - Means 'does not want to see men,' indicating a desire for seclusion or avoidance.
  • 患狐魅疾 - Means 'suffering from a fox demon illness,' referring to a supernatural or psychological affliction.
  • 本图共终 - Means 'originally intended to stay together until the end,' indicating a long-term plan or commitment.
  • 流泪经日 - Means 'to cry for many days,' suggesting prolonged emotional distress.
  • 善保爱之 - Means 'to keep it well and cherish it,' indicating care and affection.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 唐代州民 - Refers to the people of Dai Prefecture during the Tang Dynasty, a period known for its cultural and literary achievements.
  • 菩萨 - Refers to a Bodhisattva, a being in Mahayana Buddhism who has achieved enlightenment but chooses to remain in the world to help others.
  • 五色云 - Refers to a cloud of five colors, often associated with divine or supernatural beings in Chinese mythology.
  • 冯玠 - The name of a Tang Dynasty figure who suffered from a fox demon illness, as described in the text.
  • 术士 - Refers to a practitioner of esoteric or magical arts, often associated with Taoism or folk religion.
  • 道士 - Refers to a Taoist priest or practitioner, often involved in rituals and healing practices.
  • 狐魅 - Refers to a fox demon, a common figure in Chinese folklore known for its ability to transform and deceive humans.

Proper Nouns

  • 代州 - The name of a historical prefecture in northern China, located in present-day Shanxi Province.
  • 冯玠 - The name of a Tang Dynasty figure who suffered from a fox demon illness, as described in the text.
  • 术士 - The title of a practitioner of esoteric or magical arts, often associated with Taoism or folk religion.
  • 道士 - The title of a Taoist priest or practitioner, often involved in rituals and healing practices.
  • 狐魅 - The term for a fox demon, a common figure in Chinese folklore known for its ability to transform and deceive humans.

[208]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 每到时节 - Recurring event, 'every time the season comes.'
  • 通名起居 - Introduces herself and inquires about well-being.
  • 兼持贺遗及问讯 - Brings gifts and greetings.
  • 状貌甚美 - Extremely beautiful in appearance.
  • 至五月五日 - The fifth day of the fifth month, Dragon Boat Festival.
  • 自进明已下 - From the highest to the lowest, everyone in the household.
  • 皆有续命 - All had their lives prolonged.
  • 遂以充用 - And were used up.
  • 入人家偷镜 - Went into a house and stole a mirror.
  • 挂项 - Hung it around her neck.
  • 缘墙行 - Climbed the wall.
  • 为主人家击杀 - And was killed by the homeowner.
  • 自尔怪绝焉 - After that, the strange occurrences ceased.
  • 来止庭中 - Came and stayed in the courtyard.
  • 渐升阶 - Gradually climbed the stairs.
  • 坐昌床头 - Sat by Cui Chang's bed.
  • 不之顾 - Did not pay attention to it.
  • 以手卷昌书 - Used her hand to roll up Cui Chang's books.
  • 汝何人斯，来何所欲 - Who are you, and what do you want?
  • 本好读书，慕君学问尔 - Originally loved reading and admired your learning.
  • 不之却 - Did not reject it.
  • 常问文义 - Often asked about literary meanings.
  • 甚有理 - Were very reasonable.
  • 经数月 - After several months.
  • 日暮 - At dusk.
  • 忽扶一老人乘醉至昌所 - Suddenly brought an old man who was drunk to Cui Chang's place.
  • 小儿暂出 - The child temporarily left.
  • 吐人之爪发等 - Vomited human nails and hair.
  • 甚恶之 - Found it very disgusting.
  • 素有所持利剑 - Had a sharp sword that he usually carried.
  • 因斩断头 - And cut off its head.
  • 成一老狐 - Revealed it to be an old fox.
  • 大怒云 - Said angrily.
  • 君何故无状，杀我家长 - Why did you act so rudely, killing my father?
  • 我岂不能杀君，但以旧恩故尔 - I could kill you too, but I am doing so out of old favor.
  • 大骂出门 - Yelled at him and left.
  • 自尔乃绝 - After that, the strange occurrences ceased.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 贺兰进明 - Historical figure during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 狐新妇 - Refers to a fox wife, a common motif in Chinese folklore.
  • 五月五日 - The Dragon Boat Festival, a traditional Chinese holiday.
  • 崔昌 - Historical figure during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 东京庄 - Refers to a place in the eastern capital.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence describes a recurring event where the fox wife visits the capital house, introduces herself, inquires about well-being, and brings gifts and greetings.
  • This sentence describes the appearance of the fox wife as extremely beautiful.
  • This sentence specifies that on the fifth day of the fifth month, everyone in the household, from the highest to the lowest, had their lives prolonged.
  • This sentence describes the family's reaction to the prolongation of life, considering it an omen and burning the objects.
  • This sentence describes the fox wife's reaction to the burning of the objects, expressing sorrow and questioning why they were burned.
  • This sentence describes the subsequent use of the objects.
  • This sentence describes a subsequent event where a family member sought a gold-flowered mirror, which was stolen and used in a way that led to its destruction.
  • This sentence indicates that after this event, strange occurrences ceased.
  • This sentence describes Cui Chang's encounter with a child with a distinctive appearance in the courtyard.
  • This sentence describes the child's gradual ascent and sitting by Cui Chang's bed.
  • This sentence describes Cui Chang's initial reaction and the child's action of rolling up his books.
  • This sentence describes Cui Chang's questioning of the child.
  • This sentence describes the child's response, expressing admiration for Cui Chang's learning.
  • This sentence describes Cui Chang's continued interaction with the child, finding their questions very reasonable.
  • This sentence describes a subsequent event where the child brought an old man who was drunk to Cui Chang's place.
  • This sentence describes the child's temporary departure and the old man's drunken behavior, which Cui Chang found very disgusting.
  • This sentence describes Cui Chang's action of cutting off the old man's head, revealing it to be an old fox.
  • This sentence describes the child's angry reaction, questioning why Cui Chang acted so rudely and killing his father.
  • This sentence describes the child's departure after yelling at Cui Chang, and the cessation of strange occurrences.

Proper Nouns

  • 贺兰进明 - Historical figure during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 崔昌 - Historical figure during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 东京庄 - Refers to a place in the eastern capital.
  • 五月五日 - The Dragon Boat Festival, a traditional Chinese holiday.

[209]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 礼敬恳至 - Deep respect and earnest worship.
  • 当修清净 - Practice purity and detachment.
  • 当决一顿 - Give a beating or punishment.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 长孙甲 - A Tang Dynasty official.
  • 文殊菩萨 - A bodhisattva in Mahayana Buddhism, known for wisdom.
  • 狐刚子 - A legendary fox spirit, often depicted as a trickster or a deity.

Proper Nouns

  • 长孙甲 - A Tang Dynasty official.
  • 文殊菩萨 - A bodhisattva in Mahayana Buddhism.
  • 狐刚子 - A legendary fox spirit.

[210]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 每至衙日 - Temporal expression meaning 'every time the official duties were performed' or 'on official days.'
  • 邑中之狗，悉往朝之 - Figurative expression meaning 'all the dogs in the town went to pay homage to it,' where '朝之' (cháo zhī) is a metaphor for paying respect or homage.
  • 大失宋人之望 - Phrase meaning 'greatly disappointing the expectations of the people of Song,' where '大失' (dà shī) emphasizes the extent of the disappointment.
  • 取睢阳野狐犬 - Colloquial expression meaning 'to get the wild fox dogs of Suiyang,' which is used as a metaphor for dealing with tricky or elusive situations.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 唐 - Refers to the Tang Dynasty, a period of significant cultural and political development in Chinese history.
  • 睢陽郡 - Refers to the Suiyang Commandery, an administrative division during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 宋王冢 - Refers to the tomb of the King of Song, a historical figure whose identity is not specified in the text.
  • 东都王老 - Refers to an elderly man named Wang from the Eastern Capital, likely referring to Luoyang, the capital of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 双犬能咋魅 - Refers to the ability of the dogs to catch or kill ghosts, reflecting the belief in supernatural beings in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 前后杀魅甚多 - Indicates that the dogs had killed many ghosts in the past, emphasizing their effectiveness.
  • 宋人相率以财雇犬咋狐 - Describes the people of Song collectively hiring the dogs to catch the fox, reflecting the communal effort and financial investment in dealing with the problem.
  • 今世人有不了其事者，相戏云 - Modern colloquial expression meaning 'nowadays, people who don't understand the situation often joke about it,' indicating a shift in perception over time.

Proper Nouns

  • 唐 - The Tang Dynasty.
  • 睢陽郡 - The Suiyang Commandery.
  • 宋王冢 - The tomb of the King of Song.
  • 东都 - The Eastern Capital, likely referring to Luoyang.

  • The phrase '本谓诸女郎辈不随过江' can be read as 'I thought the young ladies would not follow me across the river' or 'I thought the young ladies would not come with me across the river.'
  • The phrase '今在州城上，复何虑也' can be read as 'now they are on the city wall, so there is nothing to worry about' or 'now they are on the city wall, so there is no reason to be concerned.'
  • The phrase '士同莅官，便求术士' can be read as 'Shi Tong, upon taking office, immediately sought out a sorcerer' or 'Shi Tong, when he took office, immediately sought out a sorcerer.'
  • The phrase '左右言州人能射狐者' can be read as 'his attendants mentioned a local man who could shoot foxes' or 'his attendants told him about a local man who could shoot foxes.'

  • The phrase '入令堂中悉施床席' can be read as 'he had the entire hall prepared with beds and mats' or 'he had the entire hall set up with beds and mats.'

  • The phrase '家人数十持更迭守' can be read as 'dozens of family members took turns guarding him' or 'dozens of family members took turns watching over him.'
  • The phrase '己于堂外，别施一床，持弓矢以候狐' can be read as 'Shi Tong himself set up a separate bed outside the hall, holding a bow and arrows to wait for the fox' or 'Shi Tong himself set up a separate bed outside the hall, holding a bow and arrows to wait for the fox.'
  • The phrase '至三夕，忽云' can be read as 'after three nights, suddenly said' or 'after three nights, suddenly announced.'

  • 城东有王村，村西有揪树 - Literally 'there is a village called Wang to the east of the city, and a tree called Jiu to the west of the village,' providing specific directions. Can be translated as 'there is a village called Wang to the east of the city, and a tree called Jiu to the west of the village.'
  • 使君可设斋戒 - Literally 'the magistrate can set up a ritual purification,' indicating the need for a ceremonial preparation. Can be translated as 'the magistrate can set up a ritual purification.'
  • 开函视之，我当得出 - Literally 'open the iron box and look inside, and I will be able to escape,' indicating the solution to the creature's problem. Can be translated as 'open the iron box and look inside, and I will be able to escape.'
  • 言毕辞去 - Literally 'after finishing speaking, he left,' indicating the end of the conversation. Can be translated as 'after finishing speaking, he left.'
  • 蛇既获死，其怪遂绝 - Literally 'the snake was finally killed, and the strange occurrences ceased,' indicating the resolution of the problem. Can be translated as 'the snake was finally killed, and the strange occurrences ceased.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 忻州刺史 - The position of governor of Xinzhou, a prefecture in ancient China. Can be translated as 'Governor of Xinzhou.'
  • 高宗时 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Tang, a significant period in Chinese history. Can be translated as 'during the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Tang.'
  • 金吾郎将 - A high-ranking military official in the Tang dynasty. Can be translated as 'a high-ranking military official.'
  • 使君 - A respectful term for a local official, often used by subordinates or lower-ranking officials. Can be translated as 'magistrate' or 'official.'
  • 府主 - Another term for a local official, often used interchangeably with '使君.' Can be translated as 'magistrate' or 'official.'
  • 青龙 - A mythical creature often associated with the emperor and imperial authority. Can be translated as 'a blue dragon.'

Proper Nouns

  • 忻州 - The name of the prefecture, which can be translated as 'Xinzhou.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 使君 - A respectful term for an official, used here to address the governor.
  • 劲卒 - Elite soldiers, indicating the high quality of the troops led by the governor.
  • 城隍 - City protector, a local deity often worshipped in Chinese folk religion.
  • 黄河 - Yellow River, one of the most important rivers in Chinese history and culture.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 复问庚 - 复问 means 'to ask again,' and 庚 is a name, likely referring to a person.
  • 鬼云 - 鬼 means 'ghost,' and 云 means 'said' or 'spoke.'
  • 庚命贫，无位禄 - 命贫 means 'fate is poor,' and 无位禄 means 'no position or salary.'
  • 或绊得佳女 - 或 means 'perhaps' or 'maybe,' 绊 means 'to tie up' or 'to bind,' and 佳女 means 'a good woman.'
  • 配之有相 - 配 means 'to marry,' and 有相 means 'with a good match' or 'with a compatible partner.'
  • 当能得耳 - 当能得 means 'should be able to get,' and 耳 is a particle indicating a light tone or a slight emphasis.
  • 今河北去白鹿山百余里 - 河北 means 'north of the Yellow River,' 去 means 'distance from,' 白鹿山 is a mountain name, and 百余里 means 'more than a hundred li' (a unit of distance).
  • 有一村中王老女 - 有一村中 means 'in a village,' and 王老女 means 'an old woman named Wang.'
  • 相极贵 - 相 means 'appearance' or 'looks,' and 极贵 means 'extremely noble.'
  • 顷已绊与人讫 - 顷 means 'recently,' 已 means 'already,' 绊与人 means 'tied up with someone,' and 讫 means 'completed.'
  • 当相为解彼绊此 - 当相为 means 'should help,' and 解彼绊此 means 'to untie that and tie this.'
  • 以成阎侯也 - 以成 means 'to make,' 阎侯 is a title, and 也 is a particle indicating a statement.
  • 第速行 - 第 means 'just' or 'only,' and 速行 means 'hurry up.'
  • 欲至其村 - 欲 means 'want to,' 至 means 'arrive at,' and 其村 means 'that village.'
  • 当有大雨濡湿 - 当有 means 'there will be,' 大雨 means 'heavy rain,' and 濡湿 means 'wet.'
  • 以此为信 - 以此 means 'with this,' 为 means 'as,' and 信 means 'proof' or 'evidence.'
  • 因诀去 - 因 means 'then,' 诀 means 'farewell,' and 去 means 'leave.'
  • 仁亶问何以致之 - 仁亶 is a name, 问 means 'ask,' 何以 means 'how to,' and 致之 means 'achieve it.'
  • 无讶也 - 无 means 'no,' 讶 means 'surprise,' and 也 is a particle indicating a statement.
  • 仁亶与庚行六七日至村 - 与 means 'with,' 行 means 'travel,' 六七日 means 'six or seven days,' and 至村 means 'arrive at the village.'
  • 遇大雨 - 遇 means 'encounter,' and 大雨 means 'heavy rain.'
  • 衣装湿汙 - 衣装 means 'clothes,' 湿 means 'wet,' and 汙 means 'dirty.'
  • 乃至村西求王氏舍焉 - 乃 means 'then,' 至 means 'arrive at,' 村西 means 'west of the village,' 求 means 'seek,' 王氏 means 'the Wang family,' and 舍焉 means 'stay there.'
  • 门，久之方出 - 门 means 'door,' 久之 means 'for a long time,' and 方出 means 'finally came out.'
  • 谢客云 - 谢 means 'apologize,' 客 means 'guest,' and 云 means 'said.'
  • 己唯一女 - 己 means 'already,' 唯一 means 'only one,' and 女 means 'daughter.'
  • 先许适西村张家 - 先 means 'already,' 许 means 'promise,' 适 means 'marry,' 西村 means 'west village,' and 张家 means 'Zhang family.'
  • 非意单寡 - 非意 means 'not intended,' and 单寡 means 'single and poor.'
  • 此乃相轻之义 - 此 means 'this,' 乃 means 'is,' 相轻 means 'look down on,' and 之义 means 'the meaning.'
  • 已决罢婚矣 - 已 means 'already,' 决 means 'decide,' 罢婚 means 'cancel the marriage,' and 矣 is a particle indicating a completed action.
  • 仁亶等相顾微哂 - 仁亶等 means 'Ren Dan and others,' 相顾 means 'look at each other,' and 微哂 means 'smile slightly.'
  • 留数日 - 留 means 'stay,' and 数日 means 'several days.'
  • 主人极欢 - 主人 means 'host,' and 极欢 means 'very happy.'

  • 有恸哭者 - Those who wept bitterly; '恸哭' means 'to weep bitterly.'
  • 长官不死，宁有是耶 - If the magistrate had not died, how could this happen?; '宁有是耶' is a rhetorical question meaning 'how could this happen?'

  • 一日，令率群吏，縱火焚之，遂易其墓，目即愈 - 一天，县令带领众官吏纵火焚烧，改变了墓地，眼睛立刻好转。

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 姚蕭品者，杭州錢塘人 - A standard biographical introduction, but the use of '者' (zhe) can sometimes be ambiguous in Classical Chinese.
  • 因在酒座死 - The phrase '因在酒座死' can be read as 'died on the wine seat' or 'died because of the wine seat,' but the context suggests the former.
  • 食顷，乃活 - The phrase '食顷' (a meal's time) is a bit ambiguous, but it generally means 'about an hour.'
  • 初见一人来唤，意是县家所由 - The phrase '意是县家所由' is a bit ambiguous, but it generally means 'thought it was someone from the county office.'
  • 鬼不复防御，因尔绝走得脱也 - The phrase '因尔绝走得脱也' can be read as 'because of this, he managed to escape' or 'he managed to escape because of this,' but the context suggests the former.

  • 知我能妆梳，所以见召 - "知我能妆梳" means "knows I can dress and comb," and "所以见召" means "so called me." This phrase indicates that the God of Mount Tai called her because she can dress and comb.
  • 府君家撒帐钱甚大 - "撒帐钱" refers to the money thrown during a wedding ceremony, and "甚大" means "very large." This phrase indicates that the money thrown during the wedding ceremony is very large.
  • 四十鬼不能举一枚 - "四十鬼" means forty ghosts, and "不能举一枚" means "cannot lift one." This phrase indicates that even forty ghosts cannot lift one coin.
  • 府君知我善染红，乃令我染 - "善染红" means "good at dyeing red," and "乃令我染" means "so ordered me to dye." This phrase indicates that the God of Mount Tai ordered her to dye because she is good at it.
  • 亲不下手，平素是家婢所以 - "亲不下手" means "did not personally handle it," and "平素是家婢所以" means "because she is usually a servant." This phrase indicates that she did not personally handle the dyeing because she is usually a servant.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 韦璜 - The name of the deceased, a woman who was the wife of Zhou Hun, the county magistrate of Lucheng.
  • 阎罗王 - The King of Hell, a figure in Chinese mythology who judges the dead.
  • 太山府君 - The God of Mount Tai, a deity in Chinese folklore who is often associated with marriage and weddings.
  • 镬汤剑树 - Punishments in the afterlife, including boiling oil and sword trees, which are symbolic of severe punishment.
  • 撒帐钱 - A traditional Chinese wedding custom where money is thrown during the wedding ceremony to bring good luck.
  • 染红 - Dyeing red, a skill that was valued in ancient times for its practical and symbolic significance.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase "恒与共嫂妹期曰" is somewhat elliptical and can be interpreted as "always agreed with her sister-in-law and younger sister to say" or "always promised her sister-in-law and younger sister."
  • The clause "若有先死，幽冥之事，期以相报" is a conditional promise about what will happen in the afterlife.
  • The statement "我已见阎罗王兼亲属" is a direct report of what the deceased has seen in the afterlife.
  • The question "见镬汤剑树否" is a direct inquiry about the deceased's experience in the afterlife.
  • The clause "太山府君嫁女，知我能妆梳，所以见召" explains why the God of Mount Tai called the deceased.
  • The clause "府君家撒帐钱甚大，四十鬼不能举一枚" describes the size of the money thrown during the wedding ceremony.
  • The clause "府君知我善染红，乃令我染，亲不下手，平素是家婢所以" explains why the God of Mount Tai ordered the deceased to dye and why she did not personally handle the task.

  • 太山府君 - The God of Mount Tai.

  • 君前生是江州门夫 - Reference to the man's previous life as a gatekeeper at the governor's mansion in Jiangzhou, indicating a past-life connection.
  • 恒在使君家长直 - Means 'always serving in the governor's mansion,' emphasizing the man's role as a gatekeeper.
  • 虽生于贫贱，而容止可悦 - Contrasts the man's humble origins with his attractive demeanor, suggesting that his appearance was pleasing despite his low social status.
  • 我以因缘之故，私与交通 - Indicates a secret relationship based on a karmic connection ('因缘').
  • 君才百日，患霍乱没故 - Refers to the man's death from cholera after only 100 days of life, emphasizing the brevity of his existence.
  • 我不敢哭，哀倍常情 - Expresses the woman's deep sorrow, stating that she could not cry openly but felt twice as much grief as usual.
  • 素持《千手千眼菩萨咒》 - Refers to the woman's practice of reciting the 'Thousand-Armed and Thousand-Eyed Bodhisattva Mantra,' a Buddhist prayer for protection and guidance.
  • 所愿后身各生贵家，重为婚姻 - Expresses the woman's wish for both of them to be reborn into noble families and marry again in a future life.
  • 以朱笔涂君左股为志 - A symbolic act where the woman marked the man's left thigh with red ink as a sign of their connection.
  • 君试看之，若有朱者，我言验矣 - A challenge to the man to check if the mark still exists, confirming the truth of her words.
  • 益信，因留之宿 - Indicates that the man became more convinced and decided to stay the night with her.
  • 情契既洽，欢惬亦甚 - Describes the deepening of their emotional connection and mutual satisfaction.
  • 欲曙 - Refers to the approaching dawn, marking the end of the night.
  • 托生时至，不得久留 - Indicates that the woman's time to be reborn has arrived, and she cannot stay much longer.
  • 意甚恨恨 - Expresses her deep regret or sorrow at having to leave.
  • 后身父今为县令 - Refers to the woman's father in her next life, who is now a county magistrate.
  • 及我年十六，当得方伯 - Indicates that when the woman reaches 16, she will become a high-ranking official ('方伯').
  • 此时方合为婚姻 - Suggests that their marriage will be appropriate or fated at that time.
  • 未间，幸无婚也 - Advises the man not to marry before then.
  • 然天命已定，君虽欲婚，亦不可得 - Emphasizes that fate has already determined their relationship, and the man cannot change it.
  • 言讫诀去 - Indicates that after speaking, the woman departs, signaling the end of their encounter.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 能判知名 - Means 'was renowned for his ability to judge (cases)' or 'was a skilled judge.'
  • 性刚鸷 - 'Stout-hearted and fierce in nature.' '刚' means 'stout,' 'firm,' or 'resolute'; '鸷' means 'fierce' or 'cruel.'
  • 不惧鬼 - 'Not afraid of ghosts.' Context may imply a strong-willed or fearless character.
  • 掘太岁地竖屋 - 'Dug up the 'Tai Sui' ground to build a house.' A superstitious act, as '太岁' (Tai Sui) is a celestial deity in Chinese astrology, and digging in its direction was considered dangerous.
  • 鞭之数百 - 'Whipped it hundreds of times.' The object being whipped (a piece of meat believed to be the 'Tai Sui') is metaphorical.
  • 通衢 - 'Main road' or 'broad thoroughfare.'
  • 使人阴影听之 - 'Sent people to shadow-listen to it.' An idiom meaning to secretly observe or eavesdrop.
  • 三更后 - 'After the third watch of the night' – a traditional Chinese time division, roughly 11 PM to 1 AM.
  • 明失所在 - 'Disappeared the next day.' '明' can mean 'next day' or 'the following day.'
  • 上元末 - 'At the end of the Shangyuan period.' Shangyuan (上元) is a historical era name, likely referring to the Shangyuan era of the Tang Dynasty (760–762 CE).
  • 袭封蒯公 - 'Inherited the title of Kui Gong.' '袭封' means 'to inherit a title,' and '蒯公' is a title or honorific name.
  • 有数千眼 - 'Had thousands of eyes.' This may be a metaphor or a literal description of a strange object.
  • 状类赤菌 - 'Resembled a red fungus.' '赤菌' is a term for a type of red mushroom or fungus, possibly a poisonous or magical one.
  • 宜速埋之 - 'Should be buried immediately.' A direct translation is appropriate.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 太岁 - A key term in Chinese astrology and folklore. Tai Sui is a celestial deity associated with the year of birth, and it is believed that digging in the direction of Tai Sui can bring misfortune or disaster.
  • 鞭之数百，当免祸害 - A superstition: beating the Tai Sui (whether in the form of a piece of meat or a symbolic object) is thought to ward off calamities.
  • 上元 - A historical era name from the Tang Dynasty. The reference to this period is important for historical accuracy.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The clause '选人有暴亡者，以算未尽，为地下所由放还' describes the unusual event where a candidate for an official position died suddenly but was allowed to return to life because his life span had not ended.
  • The sequence of events '既出门，逢一老人，著枷，谓选人曰' is elliptical and requires careful reconstruction to convey the narrative flow.
  • The complex sentence '君以得还，我子従愿，今居吏部，若选事未毕，当见之，可为相谕' conveys the elder's message, which includes a request for the candidate to inform 卢従愿 about the situation.
  • The part of the elder's message '己由不事佛，今受诸罪，备极苦痛，可速作经像相救' explains why he is suffering and what he needs to be saved.
  • The clause '其人既活，向铨司马说之' indicates that the candidate who was revived told the铨司马 about the encounter.
  • The action '従愿流涕，请假写经像' shows 卢従愿's emotional response and his immediate action to fulfill the request.
  • The final sequence '相救毕，却诣选人辞谢，云：“已生人间，可为白儿。”言讫不见' describes 卢従愿's completion of the task and the elder's departure.

  • 犍為參軍 - A title indicating a military officer stationed in Jianwei (modern-day Sichuan province).
  • 官舍夜卧 - Literally 'sleeping in the official residence at night,' indicating the setting of the story.
  • 大王召君 - A formal address used by the king or ruler to summon someone.
  • 身是州吏 - 'I am an official of the state,' indicating the speaker's position.
  • 不属王国 - 'Not belonging to the kingdom,' expressing the speaker's assertion of independence from the king's jurisdiction.
  • 阎罗 - The god of hell in Chinese mythology, often depicted as a powerful judge.
  • 仓卒 - 'In a hurry' or 'hastily,' describing the suddenness of the event.
  • 持诵《金刚经》 - 'Reciting the Diamond Sutra,' indicating the protagonist's religious practice.
  • 切念云 - 'Deeply thinking and saying,' expressing the protagonist's earnest thoughts.
  • 地藏菩萨 - A bodhisattva in Mahayana Buddhism known for his vow to save all beings from suffering.
  • 以算未尽 - 'Because the calculation (or lifespan) is not yet complete,' suggesting that the protagonist has more time to live.
  • 宜遣之去 - 'Should send him away,' indicating the decision to release the protagonist.
  • 忽怒 - 'Suddenly angry,' describing the king's sudden change in mood.
  • 嘉州参军 - A title indicating a military officer stationed in Jiachou (another region in Sichuan province).

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • The Tianbao era was marked by the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, a period of prosperity and cultural flourishing.
  • Jianwei is a county in Sichuan province, historically significant for its role in the Tang Dynasty.
  • Jiachou is another region in Sichuan province, indicating a different administrative area.
  • Yama (阎罗) is the god of the underworld in Chinese mythology, often depicted as a stern judge.
  • The Diamond Sutra (金刚经) is a key text in Mahayana Buddhism, emphasizing the concept of emptiness and the impermanence of all things.
  • Dizang Bodhisattva (地藏菩萨) is known for his vow to save all beings from suffering, a central figure in Chinese Buddhism.
  • The concept of 'calculation' (算未尽) here refers to the lifespan or destiny of a person, a common theme in Chinese religious and philosophical texts.

Proper Nouns

  • 费子玉 - The name of the protagonist, a military officer.
  • 天宝 - The era name of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (742-756).
  • 犍为 - A county in Sichuan province.
  • 嘉州 - Another region in Sichuan province.
  • 阎罗 - The god of hell in Chinese mythology.
  • 金刚经 - The Diamond Sutra, a key text in Mahayana Buddhism.
  • 地藏菩萨 - A bodhisattva in Mahayana Buddhism known for his vow to save all beings from suffering.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 汝合死 - Direct and harsh expression meaning 'you should die.'
  • 正为菩萨苦论 - Indicates that the person is being punished by the Bodhisattva through hardship.
  • 且释君去 - A polite request to release the person.
  • 当得永寿 - A promise of eternal life.
  • 圣容是铜佛 - A description of the statue as a bronze Buddha.
  • 头、面、手悉动 - Describes the statue's head, face, and hands moving.
  • 手足悉展 - Describes the statue's limbs fully extending.
  • 门外人何其多乎 - A question about the large number of people outside.
  • 此辈各罪福不明 - Indicates that these people are unclear about their sins and merits.
  • 已数百年为鬼 - States that they have been ghosts for several hundred years.
  • 不得记生 - Indicates that they cannot remember their past lives.
  • 何得来耶 - A question about how they came.
  • 君勿顾之耳 - A suggestion to ignore them.
  • 有恨而来 - Indicates that they came out of resentment.
  • 所用己钱，何不还之 - A question about why the money used by the wife is not returned.
  • 夫用妇钱，义无还理 - A statement that a husband using his wife's money has no obligation to return it.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 菩萨 - Refers to a Bodhisattva, a being who has achieved enlightenment but chooses to remain in the world to help others.
  • 圣容 - Refers to a sacred image or statue, specifically a bronze Buddha.
  • 铜佛 - A bronze statue of Buddha, often found in temples and shrines.
  • 鬼 - Refers to spirits or ghosts, often associated with unresolved issues or karma.
  • 罪福 - Refers to sins and merits, concepts central to Buddhist and Taoist beliefs.

Proper Nouns

  • 圣容 - Sacred image or statue.
  • 铜佛 - Bronze statue of Buddha.
  • 鬼 - Ghost or spirit.

  • 暴卒而活 - Died suddenly but came back to life.

  • 次至 - Next came or then came to.

  • 李平头钱 - Money from Li Ping, the barber.
  • 属户 - Subordinate household or household under his jurisdiction.
  • 乞追子问 - I beg to summon my son to ask.
  • 送直还 - Sent Bao Zhi back.
  • 甚无恙 - Very much unharmed or completely fine.

  • 果病 - Indeed fell ill.
  • 二日死矣 - Died two days later.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 解褐 - Refers to the act of putting on official robes, symbolizing the beginning of an official career.
  • 知捕贼 - Means 'to be in charge of catching bandits' or 'to oversee the capture of criminals.'
  • 原其僧 - Means 'to pardon the monk' or 'to show leniency toward the monk.'
  • 让靖以宽典 - Means 'to criticize Hui Jing for leniency in his handling of the case.'
  • 辞引伏 - Means 'to confess under questioning' or 'to admit guilt.'
  • 仗杀之 - Means 'to execute him with a stick' or 'to carry out the execution.'
  • 载初二年夏六月 - Refers to the second year of the Zaitai era, during the summer of the sixth month.
  • 权殓已毕 - Means 'the temporary burial has been completed' or 'the interim funeral rites have been performed.'
  • 冥婚舅女 - Means 'an arranged marriage to a deceased relative's daughter' or 'a posthumous marriage to his deceased sister-in-law.'
  • 经十二日 - Means 'after twelve days.'
  • 呻吟棺中 - Means 'to moan inside the coffin' or 'to groan within the coffin.'
  • 徐以牛乳乳之 - Means 'to slowly feed him cow's milk' or 'to nourish him with cow's milk.'
  • 公无事，放还 - Means 'you have no further business here, go back' or 'you are free to return.'
  • 肉已坏 - Means 'the flesh has already decayed' or 'the body has rotted.'
  • 可还他旧肉 - Means 'give back his old flesh' or 'restore his original body.'
  • 犬马鸡鹅悉鸣 - Means 'the dogs, horses, chickens, and geese all cried out' or 'the animals in the house made a commotion.'
  • 当有所见矣 - Means 'it seems there was something to see' or 'there was something to be seen.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 魏靖 - A historical figure whose story is being recounted.
  • 钜鹿 - A place name, referring to a region in present-day Hebei Province.
  • 曹州刺史李融 - Refers to Li Rong, the governor of Caozhou (present-day Shandong).
  • 载初二年 - Refers to the second year of the Zaitai era, which was the reign title of Empress Wu Zetian.
  • 曹司 - Refers to the officials or the afterlife bureaucracy.
  • 门卫旗戟甚肃 - Describes the solemn and orderly appearance of the guards and flags in the afterlife.
  • 冥婚 - A traditional Chinese practice of arranging a marriage between a living person and a deceased relative or acquaintance.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 载初二年夏六月，靖会疾暴卒 - The sentence structure is somewhat elliptical, but it clearly indicates that Hui Jing suddenly fell ill and died in the summer of the sixth month of the second year of the Zaitai era.
  • 经十二日，靖活，呻吟棺中 - This sequence describes the revival of Hui Jing after twelve days, with him moaning inside the coffin.
  • 身肉俱烂，徐以牛乳乳之 - This clause describes the state of Hui Jing's body after his revival and the method used to nourish him.
  • 公无事，放还 - This is a command from the afterlife official, telling Hui Jing that he has no further business there and can return.
  • 犬马鸡鹅悉鸣，当有所见矣 - This final clause describes the commotion caused by the animals in the house and suggests that Hui Jing saw something significant.

Proper Nouns

  • 魏靖 - The main subject of the story.
  • 钜鹿 - A place name.
  • 曹州刺史李融 - A historical figure.
  • 载初二年 - A historical date.
  • 曹司 - Refers to the afterlife bureaucracy.
  • 门卫旗戟 - Refers to the guards and flags in the afterlife.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 有顷 - Time expression meaning 'after a while' or 'shortly'.
  • 外传王坐 - Formal announcement indicating that the king is present.
  • 崔令传语白王云 - Phrase meaning 'Cui ordered to convey the message to the king', indicating a formal communication.
  • 金坛王丞，是己亲友，计未合死，事了 - Statement explaining that Jin Tan Wang Cheng is a friend or relative of the king and should not have died; the matter is resolved.
  • 愿早遣，时热，恐其舍坏 - Request to release the person quickly due to the heat, fearing that the place where they are held might deteriorate.
  • 王引入，谓甲曰 - The king invites someone in and speaks to them.
  • 君前任县丞受赃相引 - Statement indicating that the person was previously a county magistrate who accepted bribes and implicated others.
  • 见丞着枷，坐庭树下 - Description of seeing the magistrate wearing shackles and sitting under a tree in the courtyard.
  • 初不同情，何故见诬 - Question asking why the person was falsely accused if they did not initially agree with the actions.
  • 受罪辛苦，权救仓卒 - Explanation that the magistrate suffered punishment but did so to save others in an emergency.
  • 若不相关，即宜放去 - Statement indicating that if the person is not involved, they should be released.
  • 出门，诣希逸别 - The person leaves and goes to bid farewell to Xi Yi.
  • 卿已得还，甚善 - Statement indicating that the person has been returned safely, which is good.
  • 传语崔翰，为官第一莫为人作枉，后自当之，取钱必折今生寿 - Message to Cui Han, advising him not to act unjustly in his official capacity, as he will face consequences later and that taking money will shorten his life.
  • 每至月朝十五日，宜送清水一瓶，置寺中佛殿上，当获大福 - Recommendation to send a bottle of clear water to a temple on the first and fifteenth of each month, placing it on the Buddha hall to gain great blessings.
  • 此功德云何 - Question asking what kind of merit this is.
  • 冥间事，卿勿预知，但有福即可 - Statement indicating that this is a matter of the afterlife, and the person should not inquire further but should know that it brings blessings.
  • 言毕送出，至其所，遂活 - The king finishes speaking and sends the person away, and they survive.

  • 希逸 - Refers to Xi Yi, another official or person of importance.
  • 崔翰 - Refers to Cui Han, another official or person of importance.
  • 月朝十五日 - Refers to the first and fifteenth day of each lunar month, significant dates in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 寺中佛殿上 - Refers to a temple and its Buddha hall, important places in Chinese Buddhism.
  • 冥间 - Refers to the afterlife or underworld, a concept in Chinese religion and folklore.

Proper Nouns

  • 希逸 - Xi Yi, another official or person of importance.
  • 崔翰 - Cui Han, another official or person of importance.
  • 月朝十五日 - The first and fifteenth day of each lunar month.
  • 寺中佛殿上 - A temple and its Buddha hall.
  • 冥间 - The afterlife or underworld.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 秩滿 - Means 'completed term of office' or 'finished service period', indicating that the official's term of duty was over.
  • 寄居 - Literally means 'to lodge temporarily', but here it refers to staying in a place while not officially assigned there.
  • 大历八年 - Refers to the eighth year of the Dali era, a period during the Tang Dynasty (767 CE).
  • 患痢疾 - Means 'suffered from dysentery', a common illness in ancient times.
  • 夏月獨寐廳中 - Literally means 'slept alone in the hall during summer', indicating the setting where the event took place.
  • 長官令屈 - Means 'the superior ordered me to come', where '屈' implies being called upon or summoned.
  • 奉命追公 - Means 'pursued by command', indicating that the officials were acting on orders.
  • 不知其他 - Means 'I don't know anything else', suggesting that the officials had no additional information.
  • 疑是鬼魅 - Means 'suspected it was a ghost or demon', reflecting the protagonist's initial fear.
  • 下地欲歸 - Means 'got up and wanted to leave', indicating the protagonist's attempt to escape.
  • 前持其袂 - Means 'approached and held his sleeve', describing the action of the officials.
  • 追君須去 - Means 'we must take you with us', emphasizing the inevitability of the journey.
  • 還欲何之 - Means 'where do you want to go now?', a rhetorical question indicating that escape was futile.
  • 神乃隨出 - Means 'his spirit followed along', indicating that the protagonist was taken away in a supernatural manner.
  • 去郭 - Means 'away from the city', describing the direction of travel.
  • 陂澤 - Refers to ponds and marshes, which were absent on the journey.
  • 陸路 - Means 'land route', indicating the mode of travel.
  • 行數十里 - Means 'traveled several miles', indicating the distance covered.
  • 有府署 - Means 'there was an administrative building', describing the destination.
  • 將延之過大使 - Means 'led him to the chief official', indicating the hierarchy of the officials.
  • 傳語領過判官 - Means 'instructed him to bring him to the magistrate', indicating the next step in the process.
  • 敬肅甚謹 - Means 'showed great respect and seriousness', describing the magistrate's demeanor.
  • 事須對答 - Means 'must answer the charges', indicating the necessity of responding to the complaint.
  • 典領於司马对事 - Means 'the clerk led him to the chamberlain for questioning', indicating the official who conducted the questioning.
  • 典引延之至房 - Means 'the clerk led him to a room', describing the location of the questioning.
  • 房在判官厅前 - Means 'the room was in front of the magistrate's hall', indicating the location of the room.
  • 厅如今县令厅 - Means 'the hall is like a county magistrate's hall', providing a comparison to a familiar administrative setting.
  • 有两行屋，屋间悉是房 - Means 'there were two rows of houses, with rooms between them', describing the layout of the building.
  • 斜眼格子 - Means 'slanted lattice', describing the window or door frame.
  • 板床坐人 - Means 'people sitting on wooden beds', indicating the seating arrangement.
  • 典令延之坐板床对事 - Means 'the clerk instructed him to sit on a wooden bed for questioning', indicating the specific action taken.
  • 引囚徒六七人 - Means 'led six or seven prisoners', indicating the number of people involved in the questioning.
  • 或枷或钅巢或露首者 - Means 'some were shackled, some were bound, and some had their heads uncovered', describing the condition of the prisoners.
  • 汝所论讼韦司马取钱 - Means 'the charge against you is that you took money from Governor Wei', indicating the specific accusation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • This sequence describes the speaker's actions after the conversation, which need to be clearly segmented.

  • 隨例見王 - Formal expression meaning 'followed the usual procedure to appear before the king' or 'was summoned to the underworld according to custom.'
  • 汝命未盡 - Means 'your life is not yet finished,' indicating the person was not supposed to die at that time.
  • 放還 - Means 'let go' or 'release,' indicating the person was sent back to life.
  • 逢素相善胡 - Means 'encountered a fellow trapper with whom he had always been friendly.' '胡' likely refers to a person engaged in trapping or hunting, possibly a specific ethnic group.
  • 在生，以賣餅為業 - Means 'in life, he made a living by selling cakes.'
  • 地下賣餅 - Means 'selling cakes in the underworld,' indicating the person continued his earthly occupation in the afterlife.
  • 忻喜 - Means 'glad' or 'happy,' expressing the trapper's joy at seeing the slave.
  • 因問家人 - Means 'he then asked about his family,' indicating the trapper's concern for the slave's loved ones.

  • 幸而達之 - Means 'please deliver it,' expressing the trapper's hope that the message would reach its intended recipient.
  • 推落坑中 - Means 'pushed him into a pit,' indicating the trapper's action after delivering the message.
  • 托生 - Means 'reincarnated' or 'reborn,' indicating the trapper's new existence.
  • 奉謝 - Means 'to pay respects' or 'to thank,' indicating the trapper's gratitude.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 寶應中 - Refers to the period of the Tang Dynasty during the reign of Emperor Daizong (762-779 CE), specifically the year 762 (寶應元年).
  • 地下 - Refers to the underworld or the realm of the dead, a common concept in Chinese mythology and folklore.
  • 奴 - Refers to a slave or servant, indicating the social status of the protagonist.
  • 胡 - Likely refers to a person of a specific ethnic group, possibly a trapper or hunter, though the exact ethnic group is not specified.
  • 造經 - Means 'to create or write sutras,' indicating a religious practice to help the deceased.
  • 功德 - Refers to meritorious deeds or good works, often associated with religious or charitable activities.
  • 夢 - Refers to a dream, a common medium for communication between the living and the dead in Chinese culture.

Proper Nouns

  • 阿六 - The name of the slave or servant.
  • 寶應中 - The time period during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 胡 - The name of the trapper or hunter.
  • 地下 - The underworld or the realm of the dead.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 至夜 - Means 'at night' or 'during the night.'
  • 刘及夫人俱梦女曰 - Means 'Liu and his wife both dreamed of a girl who said.'
  • 某命当更生 - Means 'I am destined to be reborn.'
  • 天使配合 - Means 'the heavenly mandate has arranged for our union.'
  • 必谓喜而见许 - Means 'you must think it is a joy and agree to it.'
  • 今乃靳固如此 - Means 'but now you are so reluctant.'
  • 是不欲某再生耶 - Means 'are you not wanting me to be reborn?'
  • 遂大感悟 - Means 'and suddenly realized the truth.'
  • 姿色衣服，皆如所白 - Means 'her appearance and attire, all as described.'
  • 乃许焉 - Means 'and agreed to it.'
  • 至期，乃共开棺 - Means 'on the appointed day, they then opened the coffin together.'
  • 见女姿色鲜明，渐有暖气，家中大惊喜 - Means 'they saw the girl with a bright and fresh appearance, gradually showing signs of warmth, and the family was greatly surprised and delighted.'
  • 乃设帏幕于岸侧，举置其中 - Means 'they then set up a tent by the riverbank, placing her inside it.'
  • 夜以面承露，昼哺饮，父母皆守视之 - Means 'at night, they exposed her face to the dew, and during the day, they fed her, with both parents watching over her.'
  • 高问其婢，云，先女死，尸柩亦在舟中 - Means 'the elder asked the maid, who said, 'the previous girl died, and her coffin was also in the boat.'
  • 女既苏，遂临，悲泣与决 - Means 'the girl had revived, and they then came, weeping and bidding farewell.'
  • 乃择吉日，遂于此地成婚 - Means 'they then chose a propitious day and got married here.'
  • 后生数子，因名其地，号为“礼会村”也 - Means 'later, they had several children, and they named the place, calling it 'Li Hui Village.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 刘及夫人 - Refers to Liu and his wife, the main characters in the story.
  • 先女死 - Refers to a previous girl who died, likely a relative or someone known to the family.
  • 尸柩 - Refers to the coffin or burial casket.
  • 礼会村 - The name of the village where the story takes place, which is named after the event described in the text.

Proper Nouns

  • 刘 - The name of the main character, Liu.
  • 礼会村 - The name of the village where the story takes place.
